title: Viewing Clean Results
summary: Learn how to choose only the columns you need so query results are easier to read in the products table.

sketchBlocks.title: Why clean results matter
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
When you write a SQL query, you do not always want every column. Clean results mean showing only the information you need, in a clear order.

If you ask for every column with `SELECT *`, the results table may feel crowded. In the `products` table, that includes `id`, `name`, `category`, `price`, `stock`, and `created_at`.

A cleaner query can focus on just the columns you want to read:

```sql
SELECT name, price
FROM products;
```

This returns a simpler results table with only product names and prices.

Worked example:
- `SELECT name, price` tells SQL which columns to show.
- `FROM products` tells SQL which table to read.
- The results table will have 2 columns instead of 6.

That makes it easier to scan values like `12.99`, `24.50`, and `129.00` next to each product name.

Try it yourself: write the query in the SQL editor, click Run query, and compare `SELECT * FROM products;` with `SELECT name, price FROM products;`.

sketchBlocks.title: Reading columns in the order you choose
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
SQL shows columns in the order you list them in `SELECT`.

For example:

```sql
SELECT category, name, stock
FROM products;
```

Step by step:
- `category` appears first in the results table.
- `name` appears second.
- `stock` appears third.
- `FROM products` reads rows from the `products` table.

So even though the table was created with `id` first, your results table can start with `category` if that is more useful for reading.

Concrete example rows would include values like:
- `Art | Sketchbook | 25`
- `Home | Desk Lamp | 8`
- `Stationery | Notebook | 50`

This is helpful when you want to compare a specific kind of information first.

Your turn: change the query so `name` appears before `category`, then click Run query and notice how the results table changes.

sketchBlocks.title: Choosing specific columns instead of *
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Beginners often start with `SELECT *`, and that is okay for exploring a table. But when you want clean results, listing columns is usually better.

Compare these two queries:

```sql
SELECT *
FROM products;
```

```sql
SELECT name, category
FROM products;
```

The first query shows every column. The second query shows only product names and categories.

Worked example:
- If you only want to check what kinds of products exist, `name` and `category` may be enough.
- You do not need `stock` or `created_at` for that task.
- A smaller results table is easier to read and explain.

A good beginner habit is: ask yourself, "Which columns answer my question?"

Try this: write a query that shows only `name` and `created_at`, click Run query, and check whether the results table feels cleaner than `SELECT *`.

quizDraft.title: Pick the cleaner result
quizDraft.prompt: Which query returns only the `name` and `price` columns from the `products` table?
quizDraft.hint: Look for a query that lists exactly two column names before `FROM products`.

help.concept: To get clean results, list only the columns you want in the SELECT clause instead of using `*`.
help.hint_1: The correct query should mention `name` and `price`, and no other columns.
help.hint_2: Check which option uses `SELECT name, price` followed by the table name `products`.

quizDraft.title: Understand column order
quizDraft.prompt: If you run `SELECT category, name FROM products;`, which column appears first in the results table?
quizDraft.hint: SQL shows selected columns in the same order you list them.

help.concept: The SELECT clause controls both which columns appear and the left-to-right order of those columns in the results.
help.hint_1: Read the query from left to right after the word `SELECT`.
help.hint_2: The first listed column name becomes the first column in the results table.

quizDraft.title: Choose queries with fewer columns
quizDraft.prompt: Which queries would produce a results table with fewer columns than `SELECT * FROM products;`? Choose all that apply.
quizDraft.hint: Any query that lists specific columns instead of `*` will show fewer columns here.

help.concept: In this table, `SELECT *` returns all six columns, while selecting named columns returns only those listed columns.
help.hint_1: Count how many columns each query asks for.
help.hint_2: Any option that names one or two columns from `products` will return fewer columns than all six.

options: SELECT id, name, category, price, stock, created_at FROM products;

quizDraft.title: Find valid column selections
quizDraft.prompt: Which queries are valid ways to show only existing columns from the `products` table? Choose all that apply.
quizDraft.hint: Use only real column names from the schema: `id`, `name`, `category`, `price`, `stock`, `created_at`.

help.concept: A valid SELECT query must use real column names from the table schema and proper SELECT ... FROM syntax.
help.hint_1: Check each option against the `products` schema shown in the browser workspace.
help.hint_2: Reject any option that uses a made-up column name like `product_name` or leaves out proper SQL structure.

quizDraft.title: Build a basic clean query
quizDraft.prompt: Arrange the tokens to make a query that shows only the `name` column from `products`.
quizDraft.hint: A basic query starts with `SELECT`, then the column, then `FROM`, then the table name.

help.concept: A simple SQL query follows the structure SELECT column_name FROM table_name.
help.hint_1: Place the keyword that chooses columns first.
help.hint_2: After the column name, add the keyword that introduces the table, then finish with `products;`.

quizDraft.title: Order a two-column query
quizDraft.prompt: Arrange the tokens to make a query that shows `category` first and `name` second from the `products` table.
quizDraft.hint: Keep the selected columns together after `SELECT`, in the same order you want them displayed.

help.concept: When selecting multiple columns, list them after SELECT in the exact order you want them to appear in the results.
help.hint_1: The query should begin with `SELECT` and end with the table name after `FROM`.
help.hint_2: Put `category, name` between `SELECT` and `FROM` so the results table starts with category.

quizDraft.title: Complete the table name
quizDraft.prompt: Fill in the missing table name to query product names.
quizDraft.hint: The dataset has one table named `products`.

help.concept: The FROM clause names the table SQL should read from.
help.hint_1: Look at the schema browser for the table that contains `name`, `category`, and `price`.
help.hint_2: Use the table that stores all six product rows in this lesson.

quizDraft.title: Complete the selected column
quizDraft.prompt: Fill in the missing column so the query shows product categories.
quizDraft.hint: Choose the column that stores values like `Art`, `Home`, and `Stationery`.

help.concept: The SELECT clause should name the exact column whose values you want to display.
help.hint_1: Think about which column contains product types rather than names or numbers.
help.hint_2: It is the text column used to group products like Sketchbook and Acrylic Paint Set under the same type.

quizDraft.title: Show only names and prices
quizDraft.prompt: Write a query that returns only the `name` and `price` columns from the `products` table. Write the query in the SQL editor, click Run query, and check the results table.
quizDraft.hint: List the two needed columns after `SELECT`, then read from `products`.

help.concept: A clean result comes from selecting only the columns needed for the task instead of every column in the table.
help.hint_1: Use `SELECT` with two column names separated by a comma.
help.hint_2: The table name goes after `FROM`, and the two columns should be `name` and `price`.

quizDraft.title: Put category first
quizDraft.prompt: Write a query that returns the `category`, `name`, and `stock` columns from `products`, in that order. Write the query in the SQL editor, click Run query, and check that `category` appears as the first column in the results table.
quizDraft.hint: The order in the SELECT list controls the order in the results table.

help.concept: SQL displays selected columns left to right in the same order they are written in the SELECT clause.
help.hint_1: Start with the column you want to appear first in the results.
help.hint_2: Your SELECT list should contain three columns, with `category` before `name` and `stock`.

quizDraft.solutionCode:
SELECT category, name, stock
FROM products;

quizDraft.title: Show names and dates only
quizDraft.prompt: Write a query that returns only the `name` and `created_at` columns from the `products` table. Write the query in the SQL editor, click Run query, and check the results table.
quizDraft.hint: Choose the product label column and the date column from the schema.

help.concept: Selecting only the columns relevant to your question keeps the results table focused and easier to read.
help.hint_1: One column stores product names and another stores dates like `2026-01-10`.
help.hint_2: List those two columns after `SELECT`, then use `FROM products`.

projectDraft.title: Build a clean results query set
